1: apologia (apologia, 'defense'): the term carries both the everyday sense of self-defense before an accusation and the philosophical sense of Plato's Apology — Socrates defending his life and convictions before his accusers. Luke's use of apologia in Paul's speeches (Acts 22:1; Acts 25:16; Acts 26:2) frames Paul as an apostolic Socrates: a man of conviction defending truth before hostile audiences who cannot understand it. The appeal — 'brothers and fathers' (andres adelphoi kai pateres) — addresses both peer Israelites and the elder council members.
2: Hebraidi dialektō ('in the Hebrew/Aramaic language'): first-century Palestinian 'Hebrew' virtually always means Aramaic — the spoken vernacular — rather than classical biblical Hebrew. The crowd's response (mallon pareschon hēsychian, 'they became even quieter') shows the strategic value of the language choice: Aramaic signals insider status, Jewish solidarity, and removes the suspicion that Paul has become culturally Hellenized. The register-shift is a calculated rhetorical move.
3: anatetrammenos de en tē polei tautē ('raised in this very city'): Paul's three-stage identity — born in Tarsus, raised in Jerusalem, educated under Gamaliel — positions him as authentically Jewish despite Diaspora origin. para tous podas Gamaliēl ('at the feet of Gamaliel'): the standard posture for a rabbinic disciple seated before the teacher; Gamaliel I was one of the great sages of the period (the same Gamaliel as Acts 5:34). Zeōtēs hyparchōn tou theou ('being zealous for God'): Paul's pre-conversion zeal is his credibility — he was not an indifferent Jew who drifted toward Christianity but a committed one who was redirected.
4: tēn hodon ('the Way'): the early self-designation of the Christian movement appears seven times in Acts (Acts 9:2; Acts 19:9, 23; Acts 22:4; Acts 24:14, 22; Acts 26:4). The metaphor of a hodos (road, way) carries OT resonance — the way of the LORD (Psalms 25:4; Isaiah 40:3; Jeremiah 6:16) — applied to the messianic community. Mechri thanatou ('unto death'): Paul does not minimize the consequences of his former persecution; the candor is strategically honest — he is not hiding his past but deploying it to establish the magnitude of his conversion.
5: ho archiereus kai pan to presbyterion ('the high priest and the whole council of elders'): Paul calls his former authorities as character witnesses for his pre-conversion identity. The irony is structural: the very council that is now prosecuting him is the one that authorized him. His former letters from them are evidence of his zeal, not his criminality. The Damascus letters also attest to the Jerusalem council's sphere of authority over diaspora Jewish communities.
6: phōs hikanon ('a considerable light'): Paul's Damascus Road account adds the detail 'about noon' (peri mesēmbrian) — midday, when the sun is at maximum brilliance. The light that exceeded the noonday sun signals a supranatural source. Paul's second-person account (Acts 22:6-16) differs slightly from Luke's third-person account (Acts 9:3-19) and Paul's Agrippa account (Acts 26:12-18) — the differences are consistent with oral re-telling variation, not contradiction.
7: prosepeson te tō edaphei ('I fell to the ground'): the prostration response to divine presence is consistent throughout biblical theophanies (Ezekiel 1:28; Daniel 8:17-18; Revelation 1:17). The voice's question — Saoul Saoul, ti me diōkeis ('Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?') — uses the Hebrew/Aramaic form of the name (double address = urgency and personal directness, cf. 'Abraham, Abraham' in Genesis 22:11) and identifies Christ with the persecuted community: to persecute the church is to persecute Christ himself.
8: Iēsous ho Nazōraios hon sy diōkeis ('Jesus the Nazarene, whom you are persecuting'): the identification preserves the geographic-historical particularity — Jesus of Nazareth, not an abstract divine principle. The Nazōraios designation consistently in Acts (Acts 2:22; Acts 3:6; Acts 4:10; Acts 6:14; Acts 22:8; Acts 24:5; Acts 26:9) anchors the risen Christ's identity to his historical humanity. The persecutor is re-oriented around a specific, named, historically located person who has proven stronger than death.
9: to de phōs etheasanto tēn de phōnēn ouk ēkousan ('they saw the light but did not hear the voice'): the apparent contradiction with Acts 9:7 ('hearing the voice but seeing no one') is a perception-distinction, not a factual error. Greek phōnē means both 'sound' (in Acts 9:7 — they heard the auditory phenomenon) and 'voice/message' (in Acts 22:9 — they did not comprehend the communicative content). The companions heard the sound of something but did not receive the word it conveyed; Paul alone received the speech.
10: anastas poreuthēti eis Damaskon ('rise and go to Damascus'): the divine direction is specific, immediate, and geographic — not a mystical illumination but an itinerary. Anakrinō is not the word used here, but the structural pattern is consistent with OT prophetic commission: the encounter interrupts normal life, prostration and blinding follow, a specific destination is given. Paul's question — 'what shall I do, Lord?' — is the convert's first act of submitted discipleship.
11: ōs de ouk eneblepon apo tēs doxēs tou phōtos ekeinou ('since I could not see because of the brilliance of that light'): Paul's temporary blindness — apo tēs doxēs, 'from the glory' — frames the experience as a theophany of divine glory comparable to Isaiah's (Isaiah 6:1-5) or Ezekiel's (Ezekiel 1:26-28). The glory of God's presence overwhelms human sight — not accidental but theologically significant: Paul cannot see the world in the old way; he must be led like a dependent child into the new.
12: Ananias tis, anēr eulabes kata ton nomon, martyroumenos hypo pantōn tōn katoikountōn Ioudaiōn ('Ananias, a devout man according to the law, well-spoken of by all the Jews living there'): Paul's description of Ananias in the Jerusalem speech emphasizes his Jewish credentials — devout by the law, respected by the Jewish community — in contrast to Acts 9's simpler 'a disciple.' The emphasis serves Paul's apologetic: his commission came through a Torah-observant Jewish mediator, not a Hellenistic apostate.
13: aneblepsas eis auton ('I received sight and looked at him'): the healing of sight enacts the Isaianic promise (Isaiah 42:7, 'to open eyes that are blind') applied to Paul himself before he will carry it to the Gentiles. The receiving of sight is the first sign of the new creation that has occurred in Paul; the physical restoration mirrors the spiritual re-orientation.
14: ho dikaios ('the Righteous One'): Ananias's designation of Jesus echoes the messianic title used in Peter's Pentecost speech (Acts 3:14, 'the Holy and Righteous One') and Stephen's speech (Acts 7:52, 'the Righteous One'). The title draws from Isaiah 53:11 ('the righteous one, my servant, will justify many') and Zechariah 9:9 ('righteous and having salvation'). The Christological language Paul receives at his commission is consistent with the earliest apostolic tradition, grounding his apostleship in the community's confession.
15: martys autō pros pantas anthrōpous ('his witness to all people'): the universal scope of Paul's witness mandate — pros pantas anthrōpous ('to all people') — is stated explicitly at his commissioning. The Gentile mission is not a later development or an accommodation; it is inscribed in Paul's original commission. The witness-language (martys) continues the Acts vocabulary of testimony grounded in personal encounter: Paul can witness because he has seen and heard.
16: baptisai kai apolousai tas hamartias sou epikalesamenos to onoma autou ('be baptized and wash away your sins, calling on his name'): the baptismal formula connects three elements — the physical act (baptism), the spiritual effect (washing of sins), and the spiritual instrument (calling on his name). Apolousai (wash away) is the aorist middle of apolōō — in the middle voice, 'have yourself washed,' emphasizing the recipient's reception of an external act. The name-invocation links to Acts 2:38 and Acts 22:10 ('what shall I do, Lord?').
17: genesthai me en ekstasei ('I fell into a trance'): ekstasis is the technical term for visionary states throughout Luke-Acts (Acts 10:10; Acts 11:5; Acts 22:17) — a standing-outside-oneself in which normal sensory activity is suspended and divine communication occurs. Paul's temple vision — appearing here only in Paul's own account, not in Acts 9's narrative — establishes that he received direct revelation while in the very temple that represents Jewish covenantal identity.
18: exelthe en tachei ex Ierousalēm, dioti ou paradexontai sou tēn martyrian peri emou ('leave Jerusalem quickly, because they will not accept your testimony about me'): the risen Christ's foreknowledge of Jerusalem's rejection anticipates the entire Acts narrative. Martyria (testimony) is the content Paul must offer; Jerusalem's rejection is foreseen and does not negate the mission — it redirects it toward the Gentiles (Acts 22:21).
19: kyrte, autoi epistantai hoti egō ēmēn phylakizōn kai derōn kata tas synagōgas tous pisteuontas epi se ('Lord, they themselves know that I used to imprison and beat those who believed in you'): Paul's interjection — replying to the Lord's instruction to leave — reveals his pastoral logic: his past as persecutor makes him a credible witness to them. He reasons that those who knew his zeal would be compelled by his conversion. The Lord's response (Acts 22:21) overrides this pastoral reasoning with a broader divine purpose.
20: kai hote exechynneto to haima Stephanou tou martyros sou ('and when the blood of Stephen your witness was shed'): martys applied to Stephen is the first use of the term in a sense approaching the later technical sense of 'martyr' — one who witnesses unto death. Paul's confession of his role (autou kai symeurokōn kai phylassōn ta himatia tōn anairountōn auton, 'consenting to his death and guarding the clothes of those killing him') is his most explicit admission of guilt, strategically placed in the account of his conversion.
21: hoti egō eis ethna makran exapostelō se ('for I will send you far away to the Gentiles'): the divine commission is explicit, direct, and from the risen Lord in the temple — the most sacred Jewish space. The irony is total: the place of Jewish covenantal presence is where Paul receives his commission to the Gentiles. Makran ('far away') echoes Isaiah 57:19 ('peace to those far and near') and the Ephesians 2:13 vocabulary for Gentile distance from the covenant. The Gentile mission begins with a word spoken from the center of Israel's worship.
22: tēn phōnēn autou heōs toutou tou logou ('his voice as far as this word'): 'Gentiles' is the trigger-word that breaks the crowd's silence. The audience that had settled to hear a Jewish defense (Hebraidi dialektō, Acts 22:2) erupts when the universal mission is announced. Aire apo tēs gēs ton toiouton ('take such a person from the earth'): the demand for Paul's death echoes the crowd's demand for Jesus (Luke 23:18, 'take him away!') — Luke's parallel is deliberate.
23: rhiptountōn ta himatia kai koniorton ballontōn eis ton aera ('throwing off their cloaks and flinging dust into the air'): the gesture-complex — stripping outer garments and dust-throwing — is a theatrical expression of outrage, potentially involving ritual uncleaning gestures. The dust-throwing may echo the ancient custom of covering oneself with dust as mourning or protest. Luke's vivid detail suggests eyewitness or firsthand-source description of the scene.
24: anazesthai auton mastixan ('to examine him by flogging'): the Roman interrogation technique of flagellum — the whip used for extracting information from non-citizens or slaves. Luke records the Roman tribune ordering what is technically an illegal procedure (Acts 22:25 will reveal the legal problem). The tribune acts out of imperial administrative logic — flog the troublemaker until he explains himself — without knowing or caring about Paul's legal status.
25: ei anthrōpon Rhōmaion kai akatakriton exestin hymin mastizein ('is it legal for you to flog a man who is a Roman citizen and uncondemned?'): Paul's timing is exquisite — he speaks at the moment of maximum legal exposure for the tribune, not before (when it might be ignored) or after (when it would be too late). Akatakriton (uncondemned/without trial) is the legal term for summary punishment without due process. The citizen-claim precisely when the procedure is about to begin forces immediate pause.
26: hora ti melleis poiein ('watch what you are about to do'): the centurion's warning to the commander shows the chain of accountability activated by the citizenship claim. The officer recognizes the legal risk before the commander does — he has seen enough of Roman law to know that flogging a citizen is prosecutable. The command structure responds correctly to the legal signal: escalate, don't proceed.
27: Rhōmaios ei ('are you a Roman citizen?'): the tribune's question is the direct challenge; Paul's answer — nai ('yes') — is legally sufficient. Roman citizenship claims were taken seriously but were not verified on the spot; the legal penalty for false citizenship claims was severe enough that false claims were relatively rare. Paul's calm affirmation carries both legal weight and the spiritual composure of someone who knows his identity is secured at a deeper level than civic status.
28: egō de kai gegennēmai ('but I was born to it'): Paul's birth-citizenship — hereditary rather than purchased — outranks the tribune's acquired citizenship. The social and legal hierarchy is unmistakable: birth-citizenship was the superior form. Paul does not elaborate on how his family acquired citizenship (possibly through service to Rome or through the Tarsian community's civic history), but the claim is specific and plausible given Tarsus's status as a free city.
29: hoi mellontes auton anetazein ... phobēthēsan ('those about to examine him were afraid'): the fear is institutional — they have almost committed a crime against a Roman citizen. Ho chiliarchos de ephobethe epignous hoti Rhōmaios estin kai hoti auton ēn dedekōs ('the commander was also alarmed when he realized he was a Roman and that he had chained him'): chaining a Roman citizen without due process was itself illegal. The tribune's fear is now personal legal exposure, not merely administrative concern.
30: bouleusē to asphales ('to find out the certain/exact truth'): asphales ('certain/secure') recurs throughout the Passion narrative section of Acts — the legal investigation seeks exactness that the riot's chaos denied. The tribune's decision to convene the Sanhedrin is his attempt to access the Jewish legal expertise that can tell him what Paul is actually accused of within Jewish law — the same kind of referral Pilate made to Herod (Luke 23:7).
